entry.title: S/4HANA Cloud Public: availability check before posting order confirmation / avoiding COGI
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

We are integrating an external MES (Digital Manufacturing Platform) with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Today we post production order confirmations via API_PROD_ORDER_CONFIRMATION_2_SRV.
During confirmation, SAP performs an availability check for component backflush/consumption. If stock is missing, the confirmation can fail and can lead to failed goods movements / postprocessing (COGI).

Goal: Before sending the confirmation, we want to run a “live” check on SAP side for the exact component list and quantities that we intend to consume, so that we can:

  • retrieve which components are short (and by how much),
  • show clear messages in our UI,
  • block the confirmation or submit it without goods movements (depending on business decision),
  • avoid creating postprocessing/COGI records.

Our proposed flow

  1. Read production order + components from SAP
  2. In MES user enters proposed output + component consumption quantities
  3. Call SAP to validate component availability for that proposal (same logic as confirmation availability check)
  4. SAP returns available quantities / shortages / messages
  5. MES shows the errors and blocks posting (or adjusts)
  6. If OK, MES sends the confirmation to SAP

Questions

  1. Is there a released API in S/4HANA Cloud Public to run an availability check for components (similar to the check done during production order confirmation / backflush)?
    • Ideally: input = Material, Plant, Storage Location, Batch (batch-managed materials), required quantity, date/time
    • output = available qty, shortage qty, message text (or message id/variables)
  2. If not, what is the SAP-recommended approach?
    • Would using API_MATERIAL_STOCK_SRV (stock snapshot by material/plant/sloc/batch) be the recommended workaround, even if it doesn’t fully reflect the same confirmation logic?
  3. We found the CDS view I_FailedGoodsMovementItem (VDM) which seems to represent failed goods movements (COGI-like records).
    • Is it supported/recommended in Public Cloud to build a custom CDS view on top of it and expose it as a custom OData API to read error records?
    • If yes, can you confirm the high-level steps (Custom CDS View → Publish as API → Communication Scenario/Arrangement)?
    • Also, is there a standard way to retrieve the message long text (error text) for each failed item via released associations/fields?

Any guidance on the best practice for this scenario would be appreciated.

Thanks!

entry.title: Re: SAP Transaction Banking for SAP S/4 Hana 1.0 FPS03 on SAP Cloud Appliance Library
entry.content:
Hello @mathias_neid 

Thank you for sharing this information.

I’m currently evaluating the feasibility of using SAP Cloud Appliance Library (CAL) to support a fit-to-standard and business validation approach for a bank running on SAP Banking Services (BCA / Loans / Deposits) on ECC, with the objective of defining a transition path toward SAP Transactional Banking for SAP S/4HANA.

In this context, I have a few questions regarding the current availability and status of this solution in CAL:

1. The post refers to “SAP Transaction Banking for SAP S/4HANA 1.0 FPS03 on SAP CAL”, however, I am currently unable to find any active appliance or instance of Transactional Banking (CB4HANA / S4FSFND) in the SAP CAL catalog (as of 2025).
2. Could you please confirm:
- Whether SAP Transactional Banking for SAP S/4HANA (latest versions, e.g. 2.0) is still available in CAL?
- If so, under which appliance name or configuration it can be found?
- Or if this solution has been removed or is no longer publicly available in CAL?
3. Additionally, from an architectural perspective:
- Is there any recommended alternative approach to provision a sandbox/demo environment for Transactional Banking (e.g. using SAP S/4HANA Foundation + TRBK add-on via other provisioning methods)?
- Does the SAP S/4HANA Fully-Activated Appliance include any Financial Services / Transactional Banking scope (CB4HANA / FS-AM), or is it strictly limited to standard S/4 business content?

Our goal is to:
- Set up a clean sandbox environment
- Run fit-to-standard workshops
- Validate business scenarios and best practices
- Identify opportunities to reduce custom developments (Z)
- And define a controlled transition strategy without immediately replacing the core banking system

Any clarification or guidance from SAP experts or colleagues who have recently worked with Transactional Banking in CAL would be highly appreciated.

Thank you in advance.

Best regards,

Johan González

entry.title: Issue with Migration Transfer Date and Fiscal Year Closing in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud – Error AC199
entry.content:
Dear Community,

I am currently working as an SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Finance consultant, focusing on Asset Accounting, and I have encountered an issue while testing fixed asset migration.

I am configuring the migration segment via Make Company Code Settings – Asset Accounting Specific, and for my test scenario I want to perform a migration with a key date of 31.12.2025.

In my system, the last closed fiscal year is 2023. When I attempt to close fiscal year 2023, I receive the following error:

Error (AC199):  
The closed fiscal year does not match the legacy data transfer date  
- Closed fiscal year: 2023  
- Legacy data transfer date: 31.12.2025  
- Depreciation Area: 1 (Book Deprctn)

The issue is that, based on this message, the system expects the transfer date to be aligned with the last closed fiscal year (e.g. 31.12.2023). However, when I try to adjust the transfer date, the system does not allow me to set an earlier date because a migration segment already exists with a transfer date of 31.12.2025, and it is not possible to go backwards.

This leaves me in a situation where I cannot close fiscal year 2023 due to the mismatch, and at the same time I cannot adjust the transfer date to resolve it.

Has anyone faced a similar issue or knows how this can be resolved?

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: source determination between sites with Production
entry.content:
Hello,

I have a simple case with a Central warehouse, multiple production sites, each finished good being produced on only 1 of those.

With this, Finished Goods have an Internal Procurement Type on the Central warehouse ; They have In-House Production Procurement Type on the Production sites.

I cannot find how to make sure the MRP will create Stock Transfer Orders to the Central warehouse, FROM the only production site that makes the product, where the Production Model is maintained.

The system keeps on making such propositions:

  • Without Transport Lanes Priority: MRP proposes the Transport Lane with shortest leadtime, regardless of the product being "planning-valid" on the source location or on the location where released Production Models exist.
  • With Transport Lanes priority: MRP proposes the Transport Lane with highest priority, regardless of the product being "planning-valid" on the source location or on the location where released Production Models exist.

As far as I know, fixed source of supply won't help on this and maintaining each product on transport lanes is a real pain and does not make sense businesswise.

Looking forward to your ideas, thanks!

entry.title: From Manual Counting to Real-Time Visibility in SAP
entry.content:
Some time ago, we worked with a company that provides pipe manufacturing and related services for the world’s energy industry and other industrial applications.  Although the company operates in many countries and has more than 20,000 employees, the tracking of tubular components for low-carbon energy applications was still a manual task before we started working together. This meant that operators had to count materials directly in the field and then register that information later.

Tracking these materials was particularly challenging given the scale of the operation and the environments in which it took place. Tubular components were distributed across multiple plants and locations, many of them with limited connectivity. This made it difficult to capture and update information in real time, increasing the chances of delays and inconsistencies. Over time, these conditions led to inefficiencies, operational risks, and a negative impact on productivity.

Our original goal was to make the process faster and reduce errors. The most straightforward approach would have been to digitize what already existed by replacing the manual input with a mobile application. Although this would have fixed how data was captured, it would not have addressed another important issue: whether that data could actually be trusted and used in real time.

To address the situation in a general way, and not just part of it, we implemented a solution that included not only a mobile application but that was also based on image recognition. Using the application, operators were able to capture images of the materials, and the system would automatically identify and count the tubular components. This allowed the process to move from manual counting to a more automated and reliable approach.

The mobile application was not limited to image-based counting. It supported daily operations in the field, which allowed operators to execute instructions, manage materials, perform inspections, and update statuses directly from the device. Tasks such as inspections adjustments or managing dispatching processes were all carried out within that same application, reducing the need for manual steps and enabling more consistent work. Not to mention that such an application worked both online and offline and was connected to RFID readers and wireless printers. This made things even faster and reduced errors in material handling.

Deciding how information was going to move from the field into the system was another key aspect. Instead of being manually recorded and later uploaded, the data captured through the application was directly synchronized, making it available as part of the operational flow.

All this information was synchronized with SAP ecosystem. Instead of being delayed or manually transferred, the data captured in the field was updated and made available as part of ongoing processes. This meant that material movements, status changes, and tracking information could be reflected more consistently across the system, without depending on later adjustments or corrections. As a result, teams had access to more reliable and up-to-date information, making it easier to manage operations on a daily basis.

By replacing manual steps with a more automated approach, the process became more consistent and safer for the people involved. This was reflected in a 35% increase in productivity and a 47% drop in work-related incidents, but more importantly, in a process that was easier and more reliable to handle on a daily basis.

entry.title: How Suppliers Can Use the Network to Source, Procure, and Find New Vendors
entry.content:
When most companies join a supplier network or B2B marketplace, they focus on one goal: getting discovered by buyers. But these platforms are just as powerful for sourcing and procurement.

If you’re a supplier, you can also act as a buyer using the same network to find suppliers, source materials, and build stronger vendor relationships.

By using tools like supplier search and Discovery postings, you can turn your account into a complete procurement solution.

1. Use Supplier Search to Find and Evaluate Vendors

The fastest way to get started with sourcing and procurement on a B2B marketplace is by using supplier search or the supplier directory.

This allows you to proactively find suppliers that match your exact needs.

With supplier search, you can:

  • Search by product, category, or capability
  • Filter suppliers by location, certifications, or business type
  • Review supplier profiles and product catalogs

This gives you full control over your procurement process and helps you compare vendors before making a decision.

How to Search for Suppliers (In-Platform)

  1. Log in to your network account
  2. Click the dropdown on the left side of the search bar on your homepage and change the field to 'Trading partners'                                                      
  3. Enter keywords (e.g., product type, capability, industry) into the search bar + click search
  4. Apply filters if desired:
    • Location
    • Certifications
    • Business type
    • Verification status
  5. Click into supplier profiles to review:
    • Product catalogs
    • Company details
    • Relevant experience
  6. Use the 'Request for Information' button on the profile or catalog to engage suppliers

2. Use Discovery Postings to Source Suppliers Faster

If you don’t immediately find what you need through search, Discovery postings provide a more scalable way to source suppliers.

Instead of manually searching, you can post your requirements and let suppliers come to you.

With Discovery postings, you can:

  • Clearly define your sourcing and procurement needs
  • Reach a large, relevant supplier network
  • Receive inbound responses from qualified vendors

This is especially helpful for urgent needs, niche requirements, or when you want to increase competition among suppliers.

How to Create a Discovery Posting (In-Platform)

  1. Log in to your network account
  2. Navigate to the Discovery tab > Postings
  3. Click the 'Create' button in the top right corner > Create Posting
  4. Add key sourcing details:
    • Product or service needed
    • Quantity and timeline
    • Specifications or requirements
  5. Set targeting or visibility preferences
  6. Click 'Save and exit' at the bottom once complete

Improve Your Supply Chain with Smarter Procurement

Suppliers who use a B2B supplier network for both selling and sourcing gain a significant advantage.

Instead of relying on outdated procurement methods, you can use digital tools to:

  • Discover new suppliers quickly
  • Reduce sourcing time and costs
  • Build a more resilient supply chain

By combining supplier search with Discovery postings, you create a more efficient and scalable procurement strategy.

Final Thought: Turn Your Supplier Account into a Growth Engine

Your account isn’t just for increasing your visibility—it’s a powerful tool for sourcing, procurement, and supplier discovery.

By actively using your supplier network to find suppliers and manage sourcing, you unlock more value from the platform and strengthen your business from both sides.

entry.title: Re: Is possible to change the default document type for A...
entry.content:
Dear @LIUHU-01_01,

In Public Cloud the standard setup does not provide a customer-side configuration to assign a separate document type specifically for Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation postings. SAP current Public Cloud guidance states that AFCV postings use journal entry type / document type SA by default. If SA should not be used for AFCV, SAP documents that the option is to raise a WCR request with SAP to change the default journal entry type used by Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation postings to another one. Changing SA itself in SSCUI 101522 Define Document Types is possible for general document type settings, for example to allow intercompany postings but that change applies to SA overall. If the requirement is to separate AFCV from other SA postings by document type this is not available in standard self-configuration

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3519103

If the business requirement is mainly reporting, audit, or operational separation the standard workaround is to distinguish these postings by Business Transaction Type RFCV in reporting rather than by document type. SAP Public Cloud guide for analyzing advanced valuation results explicitly recommends using Analyze Closing Run Results or a custom analytic report on I_GLACCOUNTLINEITEM with Business Transaction Type = RFCV for Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation.

For example: If document type SA is used both for manual journal entries and AFCV postings the system standard will still post both under SA. To isolate AFCV entries without an SAP change request, create a report/filter on RFCV so that month-end valuation postings are separated from normal journal entries in analysis and reconciliation

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3473536

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: [SAP Partners and Colleagues only] We've officially kicked off our Product Expert Training H1 2026
entry.content:
Discover our new format of the Product Expert Training (PET) and benefit from hands-on product expert insights, best practices, and the latest AI-powered innovations across SAP Business Suite. 

In H1 2026, expert insights will be delivered through regularly published on-demand expert videos, making it easier to access focused knowledge when you need it. 

In a live executive session, Florian Heretsch, Bert Schulze, and Corina Kinzel introduced the H1 2026 program and shared the shift from live sessions to on-demand, snackable product expert videos.

 

Watch the recording here.

 

New content available every Friday!

From March 20–June 26, we’re releasing new product expert videos covering end‑to‑end processes, innovations, features, and practical project insights across the entire SAP Business Suite. 

We've published several videos covering the following topics:

  • Finance and risk
  • Source to pay
  • Plan to fulfill
  • Acquire to decommission
  • Lead to cash
  • Cross topics

These expert videos (5–30 minutes) covering product insights, new features and innovations, and implementation experiences will be published weekly on SAP Learning until the end of June. Partners will receive regular updates with links to the newly published videos.

Check out the first batch of videos and stay tuned for more videos to follow every Friday*!

*Please note that, due to public holidays, no new videos will be released on April 3 and May 1.

 

Check out our landing page for more information.

 

Looking ahead: PET live event in H2 2026

In the second half of 2026, PET will culminate in a flagship hybrid event starting on September 28 and running for approximately three weeks, bringing together the partner community and SAP product experts.


The event will combine live sessions and networking opportunities, with the possibility to welcome selected partners onsite while also enabling virtual participation.

More details about the H2 flagship event will follow soon.

entry.title: Fiori List Report: The "Process All" Dilemma — Parsing OData $filter in ABAP
entry.content:
The Problem

This scenario is more common than you might think.

In the old SAP GUI world, ALV lists frequently had buttons like "Execute All" or "Process All" defined in the GUI Status, or users could simply "Select All" rows and trigger an action. The entire dataset was right there in memory — stateful, accessible, simple.

Unfortunately, the same approach is still often requested in Fiori Elements List Reports. The reason? There's still a widespread difficulty in understanding the fundamental difference between stateful and stateless, between a classic ALV list and a Fiori List Report.

When we try to explain that there's an OData protocol with pagination behind the scenes, that "Select All" doesn't truly select all rows, or that an "Execute All" action has no knowledge of which records to process — unlike in ALV where the entire internal table was available — we can often steer the design away from this pattern. But sometimes, we can't.

The Fiori warning message helps users understand that "Select All" is only partial — it's no longer the same as it was with ALV lists.

There are legitimate use cases where, after filtering a dataset, reviewing it, validating it, the user needs to "confirm" an action or a status change to be applied to that specific result set they're currently looking at.

I won't go into the details of how the action should be implemented in ABAP — there could be timeout concerns, and often these actions are executed via background tasks or jobs. I want to focus specifically on the approach for capturing the "snapshot" of what the user has filtered.

As we said, "Select All" is not a viable option. In the modern world we have pagination, and we can't expect the user to scroll all the way to the bottom of the list. Retrieving all row identifiers from the frontend is simply not feasible.

Our Approach

The best solution we've implemented (together with @SimoneLicciardi ) is to pass the applied filter criteria back to a Static Action — essentially the same filters that were active when the user pressed "Go".

We can define an Abstract Entity as the import parameter for the RAP action. If the action dialog already has other parameters, this is simply an additional hidden parameter (@UI.hidden: true) that we can call FilterString.

define abstract entity ZA_ProcessAllParams {
  @UI.hidden: true
  FilterString : abap.string(4000);
  // ... other action parameters if needed
}

The Fiori Elements Extension

On the Fiori Elements List Report side, we need custom action logic that does two things when the user triggers the action: capture the current $filter string and inject it into the hidden parameter.

This can be achieved through a custom action defined in the manifest.json (or via a controller extension, depending on your app setup). 

The _getFilterString method extracts the $filter from the current table row binding — this is exactly the same filter the framework sent to the backend when the user pressed "Go".

function _getFilterString(oView) {
    // Table ID follows Fiori Elements convention: 
    // "<appId>::<ListReportPage>--fe::table::<EntitySet>::LineItem"
    var oTable = oView.byId("<your-table-control-id>");
    if (oTable) {
        var oRowBinding = oTable.getRowBinding();
        if (oRowBinding) {
            var sPath = oRowBinding.getDownloadUrl();
            if (sPath) {
                var iFilterStart = sPath.indexOf("$filter=");
                if (iFilterStart > -1) {
                    var sFilter = sPath.substring(iFilterStart + 8);
                    var iNextParam = sFilter.indexOf("&");
                    if (iNextParam > -1) {
                        sFilter = sFilter.substring(0, iNextParam);
                    }
                    return decodeURIComponent(sFilter);
                }
            }
        }
    }
    return "";
}

Then in the action handler, we invoke the static action and pass the $filter string as the hidden parameter:

onPressExecuteAll: function () {
    var oView = this.editFlow.getView();
    var sActionName = "<your action bound context>";
    var sFilterString = _getFilterString(oView);

    var oInvokeParams = {
        controlId: "<your-table-control-id>",
        bStaticAction: true,
        invocationGrouping: "Isolated",
        parameterValues: [{
            name: "FilterString",
            value: sFilterString
        }]
    };

    this.editFlow.invokeAction(sActionName, oInvokeParams)
        .then(function (oResult) {
            // handle success
        }).catch(function (oError) {
            console.error("Action error:", oError);
        });
}

The key point: the parameterValues array injects the filter string into the hidden FilterString field of the Abstract Entity. The user never sees it — the action dialog shows only the visible parameters (if any), while the filter criteria travel silently to the backend.

Note:  this was tested on S/4HANA 2023 with OData V4 and SAPUI5 1.120.

The ABAP Parser

On the backend, the action handler receives the $filter string and needs to parse it into ABAP range tables to re-execute the query with the same criteria.

We looked for standard solutions — for example, the method ODATA_FILTER2SELECT_OPTION of class CL_CLB2_TOOLS — but it only supports OData V2.

So we built a custom reusable class: ZCL_ODATA_FILTER_PARSER, which handles both V2 and V4 syntax and converts the $filter string directly into ABAP range tables.

DATA(lo_parser) = NEW zcl_odata_filter_parser(
  iv_filter  = lv_filter_string
  iv_version = zcl_odata_filter_parser=>gc_version-v4
).

DATA(lt_r_plant) = lo_parser->get_range( 'Plant' ).
DATA(lt_r_mat)   = lo_parser->get_range( 'Material' ).
DATA(lt_r_date)  = lo_parser->get_range( 'DeliveryDate' ).

The class is open source, includes ABAP Unit tests  and is installable via abapGit. You can find the  usage examples in the repository:

https://github.com/alespad/abap-odata-filter-parser 

This is how we approached solving this problem. If you know of better approaches, closer to standard functionality, we'd love to hear about them — please share in the comments!

entry.title: How do I USE an OUT-Table?
entry.content:
I have a procedure that has an OUT table variable:

CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE "q_FollowLinkedTables"
(
  IN  EntryNumber int,
  IN  EntryType   int,
  IN  Backwards   BOOLEAN,
  OUT Result      "q_tt_EntryMap"
)

LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT 
SQL SECURITY INVOKER

AS
  LineTable    NVARCHAR(10);
  tSQL         NVARCHAR(4000);
  ActualTab    "q_tt_EntryMap";
  AllTab       "q_tt_EntryMap";
  ActualNumber int;
  ActualType   int;
  TypeField    NVARCHAR(10);
  NumberField  NVARCHAR(10);
  counter      int;

BEGIN
  counter      := 0;
  ActualNumber := EntryNumber;
  ActualType   := EntryType;
  IF :Backwards = TRUE THEN
    TypeField    := 'BaseType';
    NumberField  := 'BaseEntry';
  ELSE
    TypeField    := 'TargetType';
    NumberField  := 'TrgetEntry';
  END IF;
  LineTable    := "q_GetDocLinesTable"(EntryType);

  WHILE :LineTable IS NOT NULL
  DO
    counter := counter + 1;
    tSQL := '
    SELECT DISTINCT "'|| :NumberField ||'" AS "Entry", "'|| :TypeField ||'" AS "Type"
    FROM ' || :LineTable || '
    WHERE "DocEntry" = ' || :EntryNumber || ' AND "'|| :TypeField ||'" <> -1
    ';
    EXECUTE IMMEDIATE tSQL into ActualTab;
    IF IS_EMPTY(:ActualTab) THEN
      BREAK;
    END IF;
    
    SELECT "Type","Entry" INTO EntryType, EntryNumber FROM :ActualTab;

    INSERT INTO :AllTab VALUES (EntryNumber, EntryType);

    LineTable := "q_GetDocLinesTable"(EntryType);
    
    /* reasonable safeguard to prevent recursive locks */
    IF :counter > 10 THEN
      BREAK;
    END IF;

  END WHILE;

  Result = SELECT * FROM :AllTab;

END;

If i call this procedure in the HANA Studio console, I get the correct result:

But I need this in a variable in the calling procedure. When I do... 

CALL "q_FollowLinkedTables"(EntryNumber, EntryType, TRUE, :Result);

.. that leads to "Not supported combination of DML operations for variable" -what? How is this supposed to be done? I want to give this procedure a variable that I can further process in the calling procedure.

We cannot process every BP manually.

entry.title: SAP Developer News for March 26th, 2026
entry.content:
DESCRIPTION

Bye Bye Build Apps

  • Deprecation Announcement (blog post): https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-build-apps-deprecation-and-the-path-forward/ba-p/14351750
  • The Path Forward, or what you can do now (documentation): https://help.sap.com/docs/build-apps/service-guide/sap-build-apps-path-forward?locale=en-US&q=sap+build+apps

Edge Integration Cell updates

  • What's new in Edge Integration Cell: https://help.sap.com/whats-new/cf0cb2cb149647329b5d02aa96303f56?locale=en-US&lastSent=2026-03-23&Component=Edge+Integration+Cell

A2A Editor

  • A2A Editor: https://open-resource-discovery.github.io/a2a-editor/
  • CodeJam Roadshow Brazil: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam-blog-posts/sap-codejam-roadshow-2026-brazil-edition-kicking-off-in-march-and/ba-p/14317926

DYK  #9 – Orchestration Client Configuration

  • More information: https://help.sap.com/docs/ai-launchpad/sap-ai-launchpad/orchestration-4953dc10c6dd48fe85f37b41109dffe2?q=orchestration+workflow+ai

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro

0:10 Build Apps Retirement

2:42 Edge Integration Cell updates

3:31 A2A Editor

4:58 DYK #9 Orchestration client Configuration

Transcription

00;00;00;17 - 00;00;06;15
Announcer Guy
This is the SAP Developer News for March, the 26, 2026.

00;00;06;18 - 00;00;09;26
Daniel Wroblewski
I’m recording this in the bomb shelter of our apartment building to give

00;00;09;26 - 00;00;38;06
Daniel Wroblewski
you some bad news. SAP Build Apps has been deprecated. Now, it all started in 2021 when SAP acquired AppGyver and over the next 12 to 18 months, it was integrated into BTP, most notably to support destinations so applications could access SAP data. In May of 2022, we started our 100 Days of Low code series.

00;00;38;11 - 00;01;11;22
Daniel Wroblewski
We had a challenge to see what the community, what types of apps the community members would create from SAP Build Apps. And in November of that year, we had the opportunity to share the stage at TechEd, the first post Covid in person TechEd, and we shared it with the CTO to announce SAP Build. And so as a AppGyver became one of the tools of that Low-Code suite and its name was changed to SAP Build Apps.

00;01;11;24 - 00;01;43;16
Daniel Wroblewski
But alas, in the age of AI with SAP Build Apps was supplanted by other tools. My colleague Patrick Scheid has created a blog post that explains the decision to deprecate, build apps and makes four key points. First of all, all development. Now all app development. No can be done with SAP Build and the tools that are available there, for example, Build Code and to create CAP applications.

00;01;43;18 - 00;02;23;05
Daniel Wroblewski
But SAP Build Apps will continue to be supported for those who have purchased it through the end of the contract. Now, existing web or mobile apps that you created with SAP Build Apps, unfortunately cannot be migrated to other tools, and you will need to recreate those apps from scratch using Build Code or any other development tool. Now to migrate back end, entities that you created with visual cloud Functions, you can export the data to CSV files and then import it to, other backends.

00;02;23;08 - 00;02;42;26
Daniel Wroblewski
But you will have to create those backends and those entities in order to import is it's been a long, strange trip with SAP AppGyver. And then as they Build Apps and I'm happy to have met and helped so many of you on their journey.

00;02;42;28 - 00;03;10;17
Antonio Maradiaga
Hola! SAP Developers. Edge Integration Cell version 8.40 is now available and it's packed with features you've been asking for. First, the NFS adapter, sender, and receiver. You can now connect directly to a network file system service or FTP2 adapter for all your EDI and B2B file transfers. So if you're doing all that file transfers, with your trading partners, this is what you need.

00;03;10;19 - 00;03;33;10
Antonio Maradiaga
Edge Integration Cell also now supports an external cloud connector. You no longer need to use the cloud connector that's running in the cluster. Finally, Edge Integration Cell also supports bulk, meaning that you can now use it as a data store. All the documentation links are in the description below. Hasta luego!

00;03;33;12 - 00;03;54;05
Nora von Thenen
As SAP is part of the A2A protocol initiative. My colleague Pavel Conniff just released a really cool new tool to contribute to the A2A ecosystem. The tool is called the A2A editor, and he developed it as an open source project funded by the EU. You can think of the A2A editor as a swagger like interface, but for designing agent cards and testing and validating the agent's capabilities.

00;03;54;07 - 00;04;11;10
Nora von Thenen
So I have part of the playground, which is part of the A2A editor. You have a JSON editor, an agent card overview, a chat integration to test an Http inspector, and more. So make sure you check it out under the URL somewhere here and in the show notes. I actually just tried it out and I find it really cool.

00;04;11;13 - 00;04;30;18
Nora von Thenen
I'm probably going to include it into the AI agent, the code base agent, CodeJam that Kevin and I just recently developed and started to give a couple of weeks ago in Amsterdam, and now we will also be doing a couple of those CodeJams in Brazil as part of the AI Roadshow. Vitaly I don't know, do we call it that?

00;04;30;20 - 00;04;59;29
Nora von Thenen
If not, we start calling it that now. So in the middle of April, we're actually going to Brazil for a lot of CodeJams all around the topic of AI. There will be HANA machine learning, getting started with, AI core, and also the new code based AI agents CodeJam. And in April or something else is happening around AI, which is the developer challenge on the topic of AI, but more to the next week.

00;05;00;04 - 00;05;26;13
Kevin Kiedelsheimer
Did you know that you can use the orchestration client from the SAP Cloud SDK for AI? To create a full AI orchestration, flow in CAP. You can simply instantiate an orchestration client from the orchestration package within the SDK, and then define the configuration for your orchestration flow via JavaScript object Notation. The orchestration client allows you to use different foundation models without the need to change the client code.

00;05;26;15 - 00;05;40;17
Kevin Kiedelsheimer
It allows you to configure templating, content filtering, data masking, and much more. You can either write the configuration directly or download it from the SAP AI launchpad and use it from file, or directly copy it into your project.

entry.title: disk writes slow Linux Red Hat 9 on Azure- SAP ASE 16
entry.content:
Hello,

We have just moved to from  ASE 16 SP03 Hp-Ux to ASE 16 SP04 on Azure with RHEL 9.

Some eventual slow disk writes at some periods happen. 

 There is  KBA 2585416 - Disk writes are slow on Linux - SAP ASE , which recommends the setting for these params:

Recommended Parameter values:

$ sysctl -a | grep -i granularity
kernel.sched_min_granularity_ns = 3000000
kernel.sched_wakeup_granularity_ns = 4000000

but on RHEL 9 , apparently these (among others) are not tuneable by sysctl anymore.

So  does anybody know if this is correct ?  If so, how cloud we set them according to this KBA?

Thank  you

Jose

entry.title: Re: disk writes slow Linux Red Hat 9 on Azure- SAP ASE 16
entry.content:
As an interested fly on the wall who doesn't have access to a RHEL 9 system ...

According to RHEL 9 / Configuring kernel parameters at runtime it's possible to use sysctl to set kernel parameters, though the link doesn't have a list of settable parameters. 

Are you saying sysctl cannot be used to set any kernel parameters, or sysctl can be used to set kernel parameters but not the 2x kernel parameters you've listed?  Is this based on documentation or runtime errors? 

This RHEL 9 / Bugzilla ticket mentions removing your 2x kernel parameters from various tuned profiles.  Curiously there's no mention of removing the ability to (re)set said parameters.

entry.title: Standardizing AI Tool Integration with MCP: Architecture and Implementation
entry.content:
Summary:

This blog demonstrates how to plug local functions ("tools") into an AI model using the Model Context Protocol (MCP). The server exposes five tools spanning multiple domains:
- Weather & Sentiment: Real-time weather data and text sentiment analysis
- DevOps Automation: Jenkins pipeline discovery, triggering, and status monitoring

The client communicates with a hosted LLM (gpt-4o via SAP GenAI Hub) which intelligently decides when to call tools. The workflow: User asks → Model analyzes → Calls necessary tools → Client executes → Results fed back → Model synthesizes answer. This eliminates hard-coded logic and enables the model to handle complex, multi-step operations like "trigger a deployment and monitor its status."

Goal: Prove that we can build an expanding library of domain specific tools that the model can use safely, in the right context, and in a consistent way, effectively connecting conversational AI with real world systems such as APIs, CI CD pipelines, and databases. 

What is MCP?

MCP acts like a universal adapter that enables AI to connect seamlessly to databases, APIs, files, Jenkins, weather services, and more without needing custom integrations each time.

It tells the AI:

  • What tools exist
  • What inputs they need
  • How to call them

Tools – callable functions with input and output
Resources – named pieces of data you can fetch
Prompts – pre-defined message templates

It also defines how the client connects (we use Server-Sent Events, SSE) and a standard way to list tools and call them. Think of it as a "plug board": you publish capabilities once; any MCP-aware client can use them.

 
Why?
  • Decouples model orchestration from implementation specifics.
  • Enables multi-tool growth without rewriting client logic.
  • Clear schemas instead of free-form prompt hacks: tools declare input schema, descriptions, and return structured content.
  • Less hallucination: model asks for real data when needed
  • Domain expansion:Adding Jenkins tools required no changes to the client logic and only involved registering the tools on the server side.
  • Security: Authentication and API keys isolated in server environment variables, never exposed to model.
  • Communication:

  • Problem it tackles:

Problem

Old way

MCP Way

New tool integration

Each new tool requires custom model prompting and wiring

Register tool once; client auto-discovers
Messy system prompts

Difficult to maintain system instructions

Prompts are first-class objects

Model hallucination on domain data

LLM invents data instead of fetching

Tools provide authoritative retrieval

Growing code complexityLogic spread everywhere

Clear lifecycle: initialize → list tools → call tool → respond

Different clients behave differently

Each client handles tools differently

Shared protocol = same behavior

Components of MCP:

  1. MCP Server:

    The process that defines and publishes capabilities.  This is server.py using FastMCP. It registers:

    • Tools (callable functions)
    • Resources (addressable data via URIs)
    • Prompts (predefined message templates)

     

  2. Transport: The communication channel between client and server. We’re using HTTP Server‑Sent Events (SSE) with endpoints at '/mcp' (main connection) and '/messages/' (message handling) to stream messages without a full WebSocket stack.

  3. Tools: Functions exposed to the model with a name, description, and JSON input schema. Example: get_weather(location) and sentiment_analysis(text). Tools return structured content the model can reference.

  4. Resources: Read-only, addressable pieces of data identified by URI patterns, e.g. weather://{location}. They give the model a way to “fetch” contextual data without calling a tool.

  5. Prompts: Server-provided starter or templated messages (like initial instructions). They standardize how conversations begin instead of copy-pasting system prompts in each client.

  6. MCP Client:
    The consumer that connects to the MCP server, discovers tools/resources/prompts, and manages the conversation. Our client.py handles:

    • Initialization (list tools, get prompts)
    • Maintaining message history
    • Dispatching tool calls returned by the model

  7. Model / LLM Layer:
    The hosted language model (gpt-4o via SAP GenAI Hub in our case). It receives:

    • Conversation messages
    • Tool schemas
      And may respond with either plain text or a set of tool_calls.

 

Workflow of MCP:

Detailed Steps:

  1. User enters: "What is the weather in San Fransisco?"
  2. Client sends full message history + tool schemas to model.
  3. Model emits a `tool_calls` array referencing `get_weather` with JSON arguments.
  4. Client invokes MCP server’s `get_weather(location="San Fransisco")`.
  5. Tool returns weather data (via API key or fallback).
  6. Client adds a `role: tool` message with result and re-calls model.
  7. Model produces final natural language answer including weather details.
  8. Final synthesized response displayed to user

Detailed Steps (Jenkins Multi-Tool Example):

  1. User enters: "Trigger the deploy-app pipeline with version 1.5.0"
  2. Client sends message + all five tool schemas to model.
  3. Model decides to call `get_jenkins_job_info("deploy-app")` first (following system prompt guidelines).
  4. Client invokes tool → receives parameter definitions: {"VERSION": "string", "ENV": "string"}.
  5. Client adds tool result to message history and re-queries model.
  6. Model recognizes VERSION is provided (1.5.0) but ENV is missing.
  7. Model asks user: "Which environment should I deploy to? (staging/prod)"
  8. User responds: "staging"
  9. Model now calls `trigger_jenkins_pipeline("deploy-app", {"VERSION": "1.5.0", "ENV": "staging"})`.
  10. Client invokes tool → Jenkins returns queue_id and build_number.
  11. Client appends result and re-queries.
  12. Model synthesizes: "Deployment triggered successfully. Build #42 is queued. I can check the status for you."
  13. User: "Yes, check the status"
  14. Model calls `get_jenkins_build_status("deploy-app")`.
  15. Tool returns: {"building": true, "result": "IN_PROGRESS"}
  16. Model responds: "Build #42 is currently in progress. I'll let you know when it completes."

Overview of implementation:

  • Server (`server.py`) :
    - Built with FastMCP + Starlette SSE transport.
    - Exposes five tools:
    - `get_weather(location)` – real weather data via OpenWeatherMap API.
    - `sentiment_analysis(text)` – polarity, subjectivity, assessment via TextBlob.
    - `get_jenkins_job_info(job_name)` – retrieves Jenkins job metadata including parameter definitions, buildability, and job URL.
    - `trigger_jenkins_pipeline(job_name, parameters)` – triggers a Jenkins pipeline/job with optional build parameters. Returns queue ID and build number.
    - `get_jenkins_build_status(job_name)` – fetches the latest build status for a given Jenkins job, including result, duration, and whether it's still in progress.
    - Provides resource: `weather://{location}` (example of resource addressing).
    - Prompts: Weather and sentiment prompt templates + initial system/user prompt.
  • Client (`client.py`) :
    - Connects to SSE endpoint (`/mcp`) of MCP server.
    - Retrieves tools and initial prompts.
    - Uses SAP GenAI Hub OpenAI-compatible model (`gpt-4o`).
    - For each user query: sends message history; if the model returns `tool_calls`, executes them, appends results, and re-queries until a final answer.
    - Implements auto-reconnect logic: if tool execution fails due to connection issues, automatically reconnects to the SSE server and retries.
    - Custom system prompt includes guidelines for tool usage, conciseness, and specific Jenkins workflow instructions (always fetch job info before triggering).
  • Auxiliary Files
    - `utils.py` – helper class (`TextContent`) for normalizing tool response content.
    - `README.md` – usage and architecture overview.
    - `requirements.txt` – Python dependencies: fastmcp, textblob, requests, python-dotenv, sap-ai-sdk-gen[all], starlette, uvicorn.
    - `.env` – environment configuration for API keys and Jenkins credentials (WEATHER_API_KEY, JENKINS_URL, JENKINS_USER, JENKINS_TOKEN).
    - `Dockerfile` – containerization manifest for deploying server to Kyma runtime.
    - `.dockerignore` – excludes unnecessary files from Docker build context.

- `YAML/` – directory containing Kubernetes manifests for Kyma deployment:
  - `deployment.yaml` – defines the MCP server deployment with container specifications, environment variables from secrets, and service configuration.
  - `APIRule.yaml` – configures external access to the MCP server through Kyma gateway with routing rules.
  - `secret.yaml` – stores sensitive configuration data (API keys, Jenkins credentials) as Kubernetes secrets.

Deployment Overview:

Local Server & Client: In local development the server is a plain Python process exposing its SSE endpoint. The client runs on the same machine and connects directly to that endpoint. Tool discovery and prompt retrieval happen once at startup; every query reuses the already-fetched tool list. Environment variables (like the SSE URL and optional weather API key) are set in a shell or `.env` file. This mode focuses on fast iteration and straightforward debugging, allowing you to change a tool, restart, and test immediately. The state, including message history, is temporary and logging is written directly to the console.

Containerization Bridge: To move from local to a platform, the server is packaged as a container image. The image bundles the code, Python runtime, and dependencies, and defines a simple entrypoint. Running as a non-root user and keeping the image small improves security and startup speed. Containerization gives a reproducible artifact: the same image tested locally is promoted unchanged to higher environments.

Kyma Runtime Deployment: On SAP BTP Kyma (Kubernetes-based), the server runs as a stateless microservice. A deployment manages replicas of the container; a service offers stable internal access; an API exposure layer (APIRule) maps a public host to the internal service so external clients can reach the SSE endpoint. Configuration such as API keys moves into secrets instead of being baked into the image. Scaling up is a matter of increasing replica count; Kyma handles restarts and scheduling automatically.

Differences From Local: Local is a single process with manual restarts and ad hoc environment settings. Kyma adds orchestration, declarative manifests, automatic recovery, and a controlled perimeter for external access. Observability (central logs, future metrics) and security (non-root, secret management, potential auth guards) are platform benefits. The application logic, including tool registration and query flow, remains the same, and only the execution environment and location change.

Client Against Kyma: When the server is deployed on Kyma the client only needs a different SSE URL (pointing to the public host). The protocol sequence (initialize → list tools → prompt retrieval → tool calls) stays identical, demonstrating that MCP cleanly separates capability discovery from deployment specifics.

Stay Tuned for Part 2 in which , we will dive into MCP integration with Joule Studio using the Joule Agent, demonstrating how AI tools can connect and operate seamlessly within that environment.

entry.title: Re: Email verification for provisioned users - IAS
entry.content:
Hello  @Yogananda

thank you very much for your support!

I still have an issue with the email verification behavior in IAS.

Some users already existed in IAS (they were provisioned earlier). After that, I changed my mapping in the source system (IdP) like this:

{
    "user": {
        "mappings": [
            {
                "sourcePath": "$",
                "targetPath": "$"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.id",
                "targetVariable": "entityIdSourceSystem"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.userName",
                "correlationAttribute": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.emails[?(@.primary== true)].value",
                "optional": true,
                "correlationAttribute": true
            },
            {
                "targetPath": "$.id",
                "type": "remove"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.employeeID",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User']['employeeID']"
            },
            {
                "targetPath": "$.meta",
                "type": "remove"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.enabled",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.active"
            },
            {
                "constant": false,
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['sendMail']",
                "scope": "createEntity"
            },
            {
                "constant": true,
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['mailVerified']",
                "scope": "createEntity"
            }
        ]
    },
    "group": {
        "ignore": false,
        "mappings": [
            {
                "sourcePath": "$",
                "targetPath": "$"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.id",
                "targetVariable": "entityIdSourceSystem"
            },
            {
                "targetPath": "$.id",
                "type": "remove"
            },
            {
                "targetPath": "$.meta",
                "type": "remove"
            }
        ]
    }
}

Then I ran a resync job, but the existing users in IAS were not set to "Verified".

After that, I deleted one user in IAS and provisioned it again.
→ This user was created correctly and the email was verified.

However, after running another resync job, the verification was removed again (the user is "Not Verified" again).

My question:
How can I make sure that the email stays verified for already provisioned users and is not overwritten during resync?

Thank you very much

Best Regards

entry.title: Re: Overhead is Zero on Event-Based Costing
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Sorry for the follow-up.

I think I may have identified the cause. In the costing sheet, I did not maintain all the required G/L accounts for the credit side of each overhead row.

For example, I defined 5 overhead rows but only maintained G/L accounts for 2 of them. I expected at least those 2 to be posted, but no overhead was posted at all.

After maintaining G/L accounts for all overhead rows, the overhead started to be calculated and posted correctly.

Could you please confirm if this behavior is correct, i.e., the system requires all overhead rows in the costing sheet to have corresponding G/L accounts maintained, otherwise no overhead will be posted?

Thank you!

entry.title: The SAP BW Migration Decision: Your Real Options in 2026
entry.content:
The question most of the Clients with BW system are asking is What do we actually do about SAP BW?

This isn't a theoretical question anymore. BW 7.5 mainstream maintenance ends in 2027. Private Cloud Edition (PCE) extends that runway to 2030—but that's barely four years away, and four years moves fast when you're carrying years of accumulated business logic, custom transformations, and deeply embedded reporting dependencies. The pressure to act is real, and the cost of making the wrong call—getting trapped in a costly rework cycle or betting on a platform without a future—is significant.

What follows are the options being actively debated on the ground in 2026. Not vendor positioning. Not marketing decks. The actual trade-offs architects and platform owners are wrestling with.

Setting the Stakes: Why This Decision Is Harder Than It Looks

On the surface, "migrate off BW" sounds like a straightforward modernization exercise. In practice, it's one of the most consequential architectural decisions an SAP organization can make right now. The choice determines:

  • How much of your existing system logic can be used as-is versus needs to be rebuilt from scratch
  • How deeply your organization wants to leverage AI and cloud-native capabilities in future
  • Your Organization’s dependency on the SAP ecosystem versus willingness move beyond to operate across platforms
  • The total cost of system change over the next 5–10 years, not just the migration cost

Option Comparison at a Glance

Option

Maintenance horizon

BW dependency

Migration effort

Innovation ceiling

AI/ML readiness

Best for

1 · BW 7.5 PCE
Lift to private cloud

2030

Full

★☆☆☆☆

Low

★☆☆☆☆

Time/budget pressure, deferred decision

2 · BW/4HANA PCE
Convert + extend runway

2040

Partial

★★★☆☆

Medium

★★☆☆☆

Existing BW/4HANA investment, incremental approach

3 · BDC Greenfield
Custom data products + accelerators

2040+

Zero

★★★★★

High

★★★★★

Transformation mandate, S/4 or ECC, AI priority

4 · BDC SAP Data Products
SAP-managed content layer

2040+

Zero

★★★★☆

High

★★★★☆

RISE with S/4HANA, SAP-native preference

5 · Non-SAP Lakehouse
Snowflake / Fabric / BigQuery etc.

Platform-defined

Zero

★★★★★

Highest

★★★★☆

Strong eng. team, multi-cloud, platform independence

Migration effort and AI/ML readiness scored 1–5 stars. AI/ML readiness reflects native platform capability, not bolt-on integrations.

Option 1 · SAP BW 7.5 PCE — The Stability Bridge

Overview

Lift your existing NetWeaver BW 7.5 environment into SAP's managed Private Cloud Edition, hosted in the Business Data Cloud (BDC). Models, logic, and processes are preserved. You get auto-patching and managed infrastructure without a significant architecture change.

Architecture

Option 1 · SAP BW 7.5 PCE  |  Maintenance to 2030

Sources feed unchanged BW 7.5 inside a SAP-managed private cloud boundary. Consumption via existing BO/SAC stack.

S/4HANA / ECC / Other sources → SAP BW 7.5 (unchanged) → Auto-patch · managed ops → SAC / BO reporting

Limitation: No new capabilities. Bridge to 2030, not a destination. Same decision awaits in 4 years.

 

The appeal

This is the lowest-disruption path. If your organization has deep BW investments—complex transformations, heavily customized InfoProviders, years of tuning—this option protects all of it while buying time. Maintenance is covered through 2030, and the operational burden shifts to SAP.

The reality check

You're essentially paying to extend a legacy architecture's life. There's no meaningful innovation on this path—no cloud-native scaling, no real AI/ML depth, no pathway to modern data product paradigms. When 2030 arrives, you'll face the same decision you're facing today, except with less time and potentially more technical debt. This is a bridge, not a destination.

Best suited for: Organizations under significant time or budget pressure that need to stabilize the platform and defer a larger transformation decision—while using the time window deliberately to plan a real migration.

Option 2 · SAP BW/4HANA PCE — The Extended Runway

Overview

Convert or lift to BW/4HANA in Private Cloud Edition, gaining a supported path through 2040. You get HANA-optimized performance, simplified modelling, and initial exposure to BDC capabilities via the Data Product Generator.

Architecture

Option 2 · SAP BW/4HANA PCE  |  Maintenance to 2040

Convert BW objects to BW/4HANA in managed PCE. Simplified modeling and initial BDC exposure via Data Product Generator.

S/4HANA / ECC / Other sources → SAP BW/4HANA (HANA-native) → Data Product Generator (limited) → SAC / BO reporting

Limitation: Warehouse-centric ceiling remains. "Better old" rather than cloud-native new. Innovation gap widens over time.

 

The appeal

This feels like a real modernization move. The modelling paradigm is cleaner, HANA performance is materially better, and you're buying 15+ years of supported life. For organizations that have invested heavily in BW and want to preserve that investment while incrementally modernizing, this path feels responsible.

The reality check

The honest framing here is "better old" rather than truly new. BW/4HANA is still a warehouse-centric architecture at its core. You're not going to be building intelligent, composable data products or running embedded AI agents off a BW/4HANA foundation the way you can on a native BDC or lakehouse architecture. The 2040 maintenance date is reassuring—but the innovation gap may become uncomfortable well before then.

Best suited for: SAP-committed organizations with significant BW/4HANA conversion work already underway, or those for whom a greenfield re-platform is genuinely not feasible in the near term.

Option 3 · Greenfield BDC — Custom Data Products + Accelerators

Overview

A complete architectural reset. Zero BW. Sources (S/4HANA, ECC, and others) are extracted directly, modelled in SAP Datasphere, and delivered as custom and derived data products. AI agents and implementation accelerators automate significant portions of model builds, semantic layer enrichment, and data product generation.

Architecture

Option 3 · BDC Greenfield — Custom Data Products + Accelerators

Zero BW. Direct source extraction into Datasphere, modeled as custom/derived data products. AI agents and partner accelerators automate semantic enrichment and model builds.

S/4HANA · ECC · 3rd party → SAP Datasphere (semantic modeling) → Custom / derived data products → AI Data Studio agents → SAC · Intelligent apps · APIs

Accelerator advantage: Partner-built tooling compresses model build timelines significantly. Pre-built content reduces blank-page risk.

Investment: Highest upfront redesign effort. Business logic re-engineering required. Strong change management needed.

 

The appeal

This is the path generating the most excitement in architect conversations right now, and for good reason. It breaks the BW dependency entirely, aligns to the direction SAP is actually investing in, and opens the door to genuinely modern capabilities: composable data products, real AI integration, intelligent applications, and cloud-native scalability. Well-designed accelerator tooling has demonstrated material productivity gains—in some cases compressing what would be months of manual build work into weeks.

The reality check

This is also the highest-investment path upfront. Greenfield means greenfield—existing models don't migrate, business logic must be re-examined and re-implemented, and the organisational change management burden is real. The question isn't whether this path is technically superior (for most organizations, it is). The question is whether your organization has the capacity, sponsorship, and appetite for the transformation effort required.

Best suited for: Organizations ready to commit to a true platform shift, particularly those with S/4HANA or active ECC landscapes and a clear mandate to modernize analytics.

Option 4 · Greenfield BDC — SAP-Managed Data Products

Overview

Similar to Option 3, but leveraging SAP's own curated and managed data products as the foundation layer rather than fully custom-built data products. Integration with platforms like Databricks is supported. No BW dependency.

Architecture

Option 4 · BDC Greenfield — SAP-Managed Data Products

Zero BW. SAP-curated data products as foundation layer. Databricks integration for advanced analytics. Best suited for RISE with S/4HANA customers.

S/4HANA (RISE) + ECC (limited) → SAP Datasphere → SAP-managed data products (curated) → Databricks integration → SAC · Intelligent apps · APIs

Advantage: Lower blank-page risk. SAP-curated foundation reduces build effort for core business domains.

Constraint: Optimised for RISE with S/4HANA. ECC and hybrid landscapes face integration friction.

 

The appeal

SAP-managed data products represent a significant investment by SAP in pre-built, semantically enriched content covering core business domains. For organizations running RISE with S/4HANA, this path can significantly reduce the "blank page" problem—you're building on a curated foundation rather than starting from scratch.

The reality check

SAP's managed data products are currently best optimized for RISE with S/4HANA customers. Organizations running ECC, hybrid landscapes, or non-SAP source systems will encounter integration friction, access challenges, or additional build work that narrows the advantage.

Best suited for: RISE with S/4HANA customers looking for an SAP-native, lower-custom-development path to BDC adoption.

Option 5 · Re-platform to a Non-SAP Lakehouse or Data Platform

Overview

SAP source data—extracted via CDS views or other mechanisms, often with BDC handling the extraction and sharing layer—lands in a non-SAP platform of choice: Snowflake, Databricks, Google BigQuery, Microsoft Fabric, or Amazon Redshift. BW is eliminated entirely. Analytics and AI/ML are built natively on the target platform.

Architecture

SAP data extracted via CDS views and BDC APIs, landed directly into a non-SAP target platform. BW eliminated entirely. Analytics and AI/ML built natively on the target platform.

Extraction layer:
S/4HANA · ECC · SAP apps → CDS views / BDC Data Sharing APIs / ETL

Target platforms (choose one):
Snowflake Databricks Microsoft Fabric Google BigQuery Amazon Redshift

Consumption:
BI / analytics layer Native ML / AI models Data apps / APIs Non-SAP data unification

Important nuance: Even on this path, BDC typically handles clean SAP data extraction and sharing. Full SAP bypass is more complex than it appears.

Key advantages: Maximum flexibility · cost control · best-of-breed AI/ML · no SAP ecosystem lock-in · non-SAP data unification.

Key constraints: Highest rebuild effort · business logic re-engineering required · strong data engineering team essential.

 

The appeal

Maximum flexibility. You're no longer constrained by SAP's licensing model, release cadence, or ecosystem boundaries. AI and ML capabilities on platforms like Databricks and BigQuery are mature, deep, and actively evolving. Cost control is real. And for organizations with significant non-SAP data to unify with SAP data, this path eliminates integration awkwardness.

The reality check

This path demands the most migration work and the deepest rebuild. Business logic encoded in BW transformations doesn't port automatically—it requires deliberate re-engineering. Even on this path, BDC typically plays a role in clean extraction and sharing of SAP data, so the idea of completely bypassing the SAP data layer is more complex than it initially appears.

Best suited for: Organizations with strong data engineering capabilities, significant non-SAP data integration requirements, or a strategic commitment to platform independence and best-of-breed architecture.

The Bottom Line: Where the Momentum Is Building

Across conversations on the ground in 2026, a clear pattern is emerging. The PCE options are being chosen by organizations that need to buy time, not by organizations with genuine transformation ambition. They're legitimate decisions for the right circumstances, but few architecture leaders describe them as where they want to end up.

The real energy is in the greenfield re-platform paths—either to native BDC or to a non-SAP lakehouse. The driving force is AI. Organizations that can see what's possible when analytics and intelligence are natively integrated are struggling to get excited about warehouse-centric architectures, regardless of how long the maintenance window extends.

The practical hurdles are consistent across every conversation:

  • Rework effort — The sheer volume of business logic, calculation rules, and custom transformations encoded in mature BW environments is sobering.
  • Preserving deep business logic — Much of the logic in long-running BW systems isn't well-documented. Migration is as much a knowledge recovery exercise as a technical one.
  • Licensing and timeline — The commercial complexity of BDC licensing, RISE entitlements, and overlap with existing BW investments is a real architectural constraint.
  • Skills availability — Datasphere, BDC, and native lakehouse architectures require skill sets that are in high demand and not evenly distributed.

The Question Worth Sitting With

The migration off SAP BW isn't primarily a technology question—it's a strategic one. The technology options are well-defined. What varies by organization is the honest assessment of transformation capacity, the clarity of the analytics strategy, and the willingness to invest now to avoid a harder reckoning later.

Where is your organization leaning? SAP-native BDC greenfield, accelerated custom build, or full non-SAP platform independence? And what's your biggest real constraint—rework effort, preserving business logic, licensing complexity, or the skills gap?

The architects who navigate this best in the next 24 months won't be the ones who picked the objectively "right" platform. They'll be the ones who were clearest about what their organization could actually execute—and built a path that matched that reality.

entry.title: Re: Custom Tasks in Onboarding and Offboarding
entry.content:
@SonamPandey@SonamPandey 

did you ever manage to resolve the "there was an application error" message?

I've also followed everything in the blog, have all of this done, but still getting the error.

Most common issues related to custom tasks are:

  • Subject User Field is not selected as externalCode.
  • Effective Dating should be: None.
  • externalCode should be "User".
  • externalName should be deleted.

 Any assistance anyone has would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: How SAP Situation Handling Automates Issue Detection & Empowers Your Team?
entry.content:
In today's fast-paced business environment, critical issues—like an expiring contract or an un-billed sales order—can slip through the cracks, leading to revenue loss, added costs, and operational delays. What if your Cloud ERP system could automatically find these problems and notify the right person at the right time?

In this session replay from our AI Readiness Service, Knut Manske , Engineering Lead for SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP Foundation, demystifies SAP Situation Handling. He moves beyond theory to show you exactly how this powerful, license-included framework works to transform reactive firefighting into proactive business management. 

Core Value Proposition: The "Why" of Situation Handling

Situation Handling is designed to recognize issues requiring attention at the right moment and provide the right information to the right person.​ It acts as an automated watchdog for your business processes, eliminating the need for users to manually sift through reports and lists to find problems.

Key Benefits Highlighted in the Session:
  • Proactive, Not Reactive:  The system automatically detects issues in the background (e.g., budgets exceeded, contracts expiring, confirmations pending) before they cause damage.
  • Context-Aware Notifications: Responsible users are informed via SAP Fiori notifications, email, SAP Mobile Start, and even within Microsoft Teams, with direct links to resolve the issue.
  • Unified View for Faster Resolution: A dedicated "Situation Page" consolidates all relevant data from different sources, giving users the full context they need to act immediately.
  • Low-Code Flexibility: Start fast with 140+ pre-delivered use cases, or use the low-code, extended framework to build and customize your own scenarios without extensive development.
  • Foundation for AI: This is the core infrastructure that enables upcoming AI-driven recommendations, where the system can suggest actions based on company guidelines (planned for 2026).
 
See it in Action: Main Use Cases Demonstrated
  • The Everyday Overview: See All Your Issues in One Place (Video Timestamp: 18:54 - 20:58)
  • From Alert to Action in One Click (Video Timestamp: 22:02 - 22:55)
  • Activate a Pre-Built Use Case in Minutes (Video Timestamp: 30:31 - 38:47)
  • Deep Dive: Finance & GR/IR Reconciliation (Video Timestamp: 39:40 - 44:15)

Watch the full session replay​ to get the complete picture, including the live Q&A where Knut and product manager Ankitkumar Kaneri  answer specific questions. SAP Partners and Employee could access SAP Partner Portal for session replay and slides access.

entry.title: How to Change Super Administrators in SAP for Me
entry.content:
How to Change Super Administrators in SAP for Me


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to Change Super Administrators in SAP for Me
entry.content:
Typically, your company’s current Super Administrator can assign Super User rights to another user via the Copy User's Authorization function within Users & Contacts - SAP for Me.
Therefore, could you kindly reach out to your existing Super Administrator to complete this action for you.

Add Super Administrator - An existing Super Administrator must provide all authorizations to another S-user ID.
Tip: Follow KBA 1614304 to copy or clone their own authorizations.

For more detailed information, please refer to the following KBAs:

2596214 - How to maintain Super, Cloud, and User Administrator S-user IDs listed in My Important Con... 

1282808 - What tasks is a Super, Cloud, or User Administrator S-user ID responsible for? - SAP for M...

If you have any questions or need further assistance, please feel free to contact us.

Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: changes to company org chart and sync with jobinfo
entry.content:
Hi @user116 

The association with Department and Division is at master data level and which wont auto update in the employee data level. 

If the existing association is removed between Department and Division and if you view the information at Job information or position still you shall able to view the Department and Division mapped even though the association between  Department and Division is removed at master data level. When you try to edit the position or job information then you shall able to view Division and not the department. During edit action the associations are validated. 

New association will work at "on-change" action. So you have to either re-import or manually update the department information in position or job information.

 

Thanks & Regards

Arun

entry.title: Re: source determination between sites with Production
entry.content:
There are priorities on the transport lanes. And you may add products to the transport lanes. With a couple of these you should be able to fullfil your wish.

You can also use different transport zones that may help you also. This may be needed to be configured in the business configuration.

Klaus

entry.title: Re: Best Practice for Sales Service with Inbound Material...
entry.content: Yes, the system allows you to reference the original service sales order when creating a returns order, so the documents are technically linked. But the process logic will always treat it as a return (credit), not as a chargeable inbound service. For your waste collection scenario, the fit‑to‑standard approach remains to use J14 for service billing and handle inbound postings separately.
